title: AI Is Making Engineering Faster But Not Better. Here Is How to Fix That.
excerpt: AI coding tools are boosting output metrics across the board, but incidents, security flaws, and change failure rates are climbing just as fast. The real question is not whether to adopt AI, but how to adopt it without sacrificing the quality that keeps your product alive.
content:
There is a strange paradox unfolding in engineering organizations right now. AI coding assistants are everywhere. PRs per author are up 20% year over year. Engineers report feeling more productive than ever. And yet, incidents per PR are up 23.5%, change failure rates have climbed roughly 30%, and security vulnerabilities in AI-co-authored code are nearly 3x higher than in human-written code.

We are shipping faster. We are not shipping better.

## The Perception Gap

One of the most striking findings from recent research is that experienced developers using AI tools were actually 19% slower on complex tasks, despite believing they were 20% faster. That perception gap should concern every engineering leader. When your team believes a tool is helping while the data suggests otherwise, you have a blind spot that compounds over time.

This is not an argument against AI coding tools. I use them daily and they genuinely accelerate certain workflows. But there is a meaningful difference between "faster at generating code" and "faster at delivering reliable software." Those two things are not the same, and conflating them is where organizations get into trouble.

## Where AI Excels and Where It Fails

AI assistants are excellent at boilerplate, scaffolding, and pattern completion. They reduce the friction of getting started. They are less reliable at understanding system-wide implications, security boundaries, and the subtle design decisions that separate maintainable code from technical debt.

The challenge is that boilerplate and scaffolding are the easy parts of software engineering. The hard parts, the decisions about architecture, error handling, security, and how systems interact under failure conditions, are exactly where AI tools introduce the most risk.

## A Framework for AI-Assisted Quality

After working through this transition with my own teams, I have landed on a framework that keeps AI's speed benefits while protecting quality.

### 1. Treat AI Output as a First Draft

Establish a team norm that AI-generated code gets the same scrutiny as a junior engineer's first PR. This is not a slight against AI. It is an acknowledgment that generated code optimizes for local correctness, not system-level coherence. The review process should focus on integration points, edge cases, and security implications.

### 2. Invest in Testing Infrastructure, Not Just Generation

If AI is generating code 3x faster, your testing infrastructure needs to scale proportionally. This means automated security scanning in CI, property-based testing for edge cases, and integration test suites that catch the cross-boundary issues AI tends to miss. The teams that win in this era are the ones where the test suite is the safety net, not the code review.

### 3. Measure What Matters

Stop celebrating velocity metrics in isolation. Track change failure rate alongside deployment frequency. Monitor mean time to recovery alongside throughput. If your speed is going up but your reliability is going down, you are not more productive. You are just generating more work for your future self.

### 4. Redefine the Senior Engineer's Role

With AI handling more generation work, senior engineers become more valuable as system thinkers and quality gatekeepers, not less. The ability to look at generated code and ask "what happens when this service is down?" or "does this handle the race condition in our payment flow?" is worth more now than it was a year ago.

## The Uncomfortable Truth

The organizations that will lead in 2026 and beyond are not the ones that adopted AI the fastest. They are the ones that paired AI adoption with a proportional investment in quality systems. Speed without quality is just faster failure.

Every tool amplifies what you already are. If your engineering culture values careful design and thorough testing, AI tools make you faster at building reliable software. If your culture optimizes primarily for shipping speed, AI tools make you faster at generating technical debt.

The choice is not whether to use AI. That ship has sailed. The choice is whether you build the quality infrastructure to use it responsibly.

title: From Velocity to Value: Rethinking What Engineering Effectiveness Actually Means
excerpt: DORA metrics and sprint velocity were supposed to tell us how productive our teams are. Instead, they taught us that measuring developer output is fundamentally harder than measuring factory output, and that the best engineers often create value by writing less code, not more.
content:
Every VP of Engineering eventually faces the same uncomfortable question from the board or the CEO: "How do we know our engineering team is productive?" And every VP of Engineering who has been in the seat long enough knows that the honest answer is more nuanced than anyone in that room wants to hear.

We have spent the last decade trying to answer this question with metrics. DORA gave us deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, and mean time to recovery. Agile gave us story points and sprint velocity. And while these frameworks have genuine value, they have also revealed a deeper problem: measuring developer productivity is fundamentally different from measuring factory output, and most of our tools were designed for the factory.

## The Goodhart's Law Problem

When you make deployment frequency a target, teams deploy more frequently. That sounds good until you realize they are deploying smaller, less meaningful changes to hit the number. When you track lines of code, you get verbose code. When you measure PR throughput, you get smaller PRs that may not represent meaningful units of work.

This is Goodhart's Law playing out in real time: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. And in engineering, where the work is creative and cognitive, this effect is amplified.

## What the Best Teams Actually Measure

The organizations I have seen navigate this most effectively have shifted from measuring output to measuring effectiveness. That distinction matters. Output asks "how much did we produce?" Effectiveness asks "did we produce the right things, and did they work?"

In practice, this means tracking a different set of signals.

### Developer Experience Metrics

Flow state frequency, cognitive load assessments, and developer satisfaction surveys tell you about the conditions that enable good work. If your developers are spending 40% of their time on environment setup, debugging CI, or navigating legacy code without documentation, no amount of output measurement will fix the underlying problem.

### Business Impact Traceability

Can you trace a feature from deployment to business outcome? Not in theory, but in practice with real data. The best engineering organizations I have worked with can tell you not just that they shipped 47 features last quarter, but that Feature X drove a 12% increase in retention for the target segment. This closes the loop between engineering work and business value in a way that velocity metrics never can.

### Architectural Health

Technical debt is invisible in sprint metrics until it is catastrophic. Leading indicators like dependency freshness, test coverage trends, and the ratio of planned work to unplanned work give you a view into whether your team is building on solid ground or accumulating hidden risk.

### Incident Learning Rate

Rather than just tracking mean time to recovery, track how often the same category of incident recurs. A team that fixes incidents quickly but keeps having the same types of failures is optimizing for response, not for resilience. The learning rate, how quickly systemic issues get permanently resolved, is a much better signal of engineering maturity.

## The AI Productivity Paradox

This conversation has become even more relevant with the rise of AI coding assistants. Individual output metrics are going up, but organization-wide productivity gains are not scaling at the same rate. The 2025 DORA report highlighted this paradox explicitly.

The reason is that most AI tools optimize for code generation, which is only one part of the software development lifecycle. Design, review, testing, deployment, monitoring, and incident response are all part of the equation. Speeding up generation without proportionally improving the rest of the pipeline creates bottlenecks downstream.

## A Practical Shift for Engineering Leaders

If you are a VP of Engineering or CTO trying to navigate this, here is what I would recommend.

First, stop reporting velocity metrics to the board. Instead, report on business outcomes your team enabled, architectural investments that reduced risk, and developer satisfaction trends. These tell a more accurate story about your team's health and impact.

Second, invest in developer experience as a first-class concern. Every hour your engineers spend fighting tooling is an hour they are not spending on the work that differentiates your product. Platform engineering, internal developer portals, and streamlined CI/CD are not overhead. They are force multipliers.

Third, accept that the best engineers often create value by writing less code, not more. The senior engineer who prevents a bad architectural decision, who simplifies a complex system, or who mentors a junior engineer through a tricky problem is creating enormous value that will never show up in a PR dashboard.

## The Deeper Question

Ultimately, the question is not "how do we measure developer productivity?" The question is "are we building the right things, with the right quality, at a sustainable pace?" That is a harder question, and it does not have a single-number answer. But it is the right question, and the leaders who embrace its complexity will build better organizations than those who chase simpler metrics.

title: The Senior-Only Team Trap: Why Freezing Junior Hiring Will Cost You More Than It Saves
excerpt: As AI handles more foundational engineering tasks, many organizations are quietly freezing junior hiring. This is a strategic mistake that trades short-term efficiency for long-term organizational fragility.
content:
There is a trend I have been watching with growing concern. As AI coding assistants become more capable, engineering organizations are quietly freezing entry-level hiring. The logic seems sound on the surface: if AI can handle boilerplate, unit tests, and documentation, why hire junior engineers to do that work?

I believe this is one of the most significant strategic mistakes an engineering organization can make right now. Not because the premise is wrong (AI can genuinely handle many foundational tasks), but because the conclusion misunderstands what junior engineers actually contribute to an organization.

## The Visible and Invisible Value of Junior Engineers

The visible value of junior engineers is their output: the code they write, the bugs they fix, the features they ship. And yes, AI is getting better at these tasks.

The invisible value is much harder to replace. Junior engineers create teaching pressure that forces senior engineers to articulate their reasoning. They challenge assumptions because they are not yet conditioned to accept "that's how we've always done it." They become your mid-level and senior engineers in 2-3 years, carrying deep institutional knowledge that no amount of documentation can replicate.

When you stop hiring juniors, you do not just lose entry-level output. You lose the pipeline that produces your future technical leaders.

## The Compounding Cost

Organizations that freeze junior hiring for 2-3 years face a compounding problem. By year three, your "mid-level" pipeline is empty. Your senior engineers have not mentored anyone, so their own leadership skills have atrophied. And you are now competing for experienced hires in a market where every other company that made the same mistake is also desperately trying to backfill.

I have seen this pattern before. During the 2008 recession, many companies froze campus hiring. By 2012-2014, those same companies were paying premium salaries for mid-level engineers they could have developed internally at a fraction of the cost. The talent gap took nearly a decade to fully resolve at some organizations.

## The AI Augmented Junior Engineer

The smarter play is not to eliminate junior roles but to redefine them. AI changes what junior engineers do, not whether you need them.

In the teams I lead, we have repositioned early-career engineers from "code generators" to "system verifiers." Their job is not to write boilerplate from scratch. It is to use AI tools to generate initial implementations and then rigorously verify the output against our architecture, security requirements, and business logic.

This is actually a more demanding and more educational role than traditional junior engineering. Instead of learning to write for loops, they are learning to evaluate AI output, identify security vulnerabilities, and understand system design from day one. They are developing senior-level judgment earlier in their careers.

## The Mentorship Multiplier

There is another angle that gets overlooked. Senior engineers who mentor others become better engineers themselves. The act of explaining why a particular architectural pattern was chosen, or why a certain security boundary matters, forces a depth of understanding that passive knowledge does not.

When you remove mentorship from a senior engineer's responsibilities, you remove one of the most effective mechanisms for deepening their own expertise. Over time, this leads to a team of experienced practitioners who can execute but struggle to innovate because they have never had to articulate the "why" behind their decisions.

## What I Recommend to Other Leaders

If you are leading an engineering organization right now, here is my perspective on talent strategy.

### Redefine Junior Roles, Do Not Eliminate Them

Create explicit "AI-augmented" role descriptions that focus on verification, testing, and system understanding rather than raw code generation. Set expectations that junior engineers will use AI tools from day one, and evaluate them on the quality of their judgment, not the volume of their output.

### Build Structured Mentorship Programs

Do not leave mentorship to chance. Create formal pairing programs where senior engineers are responsible for developing junior talent. Make it part of their performance evaluation. The organizations that formalize this will have a talent advantage in 2-3 years.

### Invest in Evaluation Skills

The most valuable engineering skill in an AI-augmented world is the ability to evaluate and verify output you did not write. This applies to junior and senior engineers alike. Train your entire organization on how to critically assess AI-generated code, including security review, performance implications, and architectural coherence.

### Measure What Junior Engineers Actually Contribute

Track the less visible contributions: questions asked that revealed hidden assumptions, reviews that caught AI-generated security flaws, and improvements to documentation and onboarding materials. These create organizational value that goes well beyond individual output.

## The Long View

Every decision about talent has a time horizon. The senior-only team feels efficient today because you are capturing the benefits without yet paying the costs. Those costs, an empty pipeline, a mentorship deficit, and a premium on external hiring, will come due in 2-3 years.

The organizations that will have the strongest engineering cultures in 2028 and beyond are the ones investing in junior talent today, not despite AI, but because of it. AI changes the shape of the work, but it does not change the fundamental truth that great engineering organizations are built on continuous talent development.

The question is not whether you can afford to keep hiring juniors. The question is whether you can afford not to.
